*M. H. Sufi .. .. Bombay.

*Nominated candidates.

INDIAN POLICE.

Appointments to the Indian Police from the United Kingdom are made by written competitive examination held annually in June. The examination is confined to male British subjects of non-Asiatic domicile who are over 19 and under 21 years of age on the 1st August of the year of competition and are unmarried.

Successful candidates are appointed on probation and are required to pass certain prescribed departmental examinations within two years. They are required to provide themselves with uniform, and the India Office makes a grant of £30 towards the cost of this.

On arrival in India probationers are attached to the Central Police Training School of the Province to which they have been allotted. At these Training Schools they receive practical instruction in the duties of a police officer, and are given facilities for the study of Criminal Law and Procedure and the vernaculars of the Province.

PAY.

Basic pay is paid monthly in rupees : overseas pay is paid in sterling from the fifth year onwards. Pay, including overseas pay, is regulated by a time-scale and ranges from £405 per annum on first appointment to £1,575 per annum in the twenty-sixth year of service. In addition, there are a number of posts above the time-scale carrying pay between £1,665 and £2,860 per annum.

LEAVE.

An officer earns every year rather more than 2½ months' leave on full pay, or 5 months' on half-pay. He cannot take leave out of India every year, but leave can be accumulated.

PASSAGES.

In addition to a free passage to India on first appointment, an officer is entitled to four free passages to and from India at first-class rates for himself, and the same number for his wife. He can, if he prefers to do so, travel at a cheaper rate and utilise the savings for making additional journeys.

PENSION.

Retirement is ordinarily required at the age of 55 years. An officer becomes eligible for full pension (£612 10s. per annum) after 30 years' qualifying service; he may, however, be permitted to retire voluntarily on a pension not exceeding £525 per annum, after 25 years' qualifying service. Special additional pensions ranging from £26 to £218 per annum may be earned by service in a number of the higher posts carrying special responsibilities. Provision also exists under which an officer may retire at any time after completing 5 years' service, for reasons other than ill-health, on a proportionate pension based on his length of active service.

An officer who is invalided may be granted a pension not exceeding £175 per annum after 7 years qualifying service, up to £595 per annum after 29 years qualifying service. If he has less than 7 years' qualifying service, he receives a gratuity of one month's emoluments for each completed year of qualifying service.

PROVIDENT FUND AND FAMILY PENSION FUND.

Subscription to the General Provident Fund is compulsory up to 6½ per cent. of salary; the amount thus subscribed plus interest is the absolute property of the officer. Subscriptions to this Fund may, however, be reduced by the amount of the subscription to the Family Pension Fund, to which all officers must contribute.

Further information and forms of application may be obtained from the Secretary, Services and General Department, India Office, Whitehall, S.W.I.

Entry into the Indian Police in India is confined to candidates of Indian domicile. Forms of application together with Rules, Regulations and Syllabus of the Examination may be obtained from the Government of the Province to which the prospective candidate belongs, or if he is a subject of an Indian State, from the Political Officer or Agent.

INDIAN ECCLESIASTICAL ESTABLISHMENT
(CHURCH OF INDIA, BURMA AND CEYLON).

1. Appointments of Chaplains to this Establishment are made by the Secretary of State for India on the recommendation of the India and Burma Chaplaincies Board of the Church of England.

2. Candidates for these appointments, which are made as vacancies occur, must be Priests who have been for at least three years in Holy Orders and should ordinarily be between the ages of twenty-seven and thirty-four years.

3. Applications for nominations should be submitted to the Secretary, India and Burma Chaplaincies Board: Sir Miles Irving, C.I.E., O.B.E., Bayswater Farm, Headington, Oxford.

A memorandum giving the conditions of service together with forms of application may be obtained from—The Secretary, Public and Judicial Department, India Office, Whitehall, S.W.1.

4. An Ecclesiastical Establishment is maintained in Burma as well as in India (see page 194) and a candidate may indicate on his application form whether he also desires to be considered for the Burma Ecclesiastical Establishment. Priests appointed to the Indian or Burma Ecclesiastical Establishments are not liable to serve in Ceylon.

INDIAN ECCLESIASTICAL ESTABLISHMENT
(CHURCH OF SCOTLAND).

1. Appointments of Chaplains to the Indian Ecclesiastical Establishment (Church of Scotland) are made by the Secretary of State for India on the recommendation of the Colonial and Continental Committee of the Church of Scotland.

2. Candidates for these appointments must have been licensed for three years and, ordinarily, they should be under thirty-four years of age.

3. Applications should be submitted to the Secretary, The Colonial and Continental Committee of the Church of Scotland, 121, George Street, Edinburgh.

4. A memorandum giving the conditions of service may be obtained from the Secretary, Public and Judicial Department, India Office Whitehall, S.W.1.

AGRICULTURAL, EDUCATIONAL, FOREST, ENGINEERING
AND VETERINARY SERVICES.

The above Services are now under the control of the Government of India and the Provincial Governments, and appointments to them are no longer made by the Secretary of State for India. Any recruitment in respect of them that may be undertaken in England is normally conducted by the High Commissioner for India, India House, Aldwych, London, W.C.2, to whom any enquiries should be addressed.

Applications for appointments for which the High Commissioner may be asked to recruit are ordinarily invited by advertisement in the Press. Such appointments occur only at irregular intervals. Particulars as to any Rules issued by the Governments in India, regulating the Services in question, may be obtained from the High Commissioner.

GEOLOGICAL SURVEY, INDIAN CUSTOMS SERVICE, INDIAN
AUDIT AND ACCOUNTS SERVICE, INDIAN RAILWAY
ACCOUNTS SERVICE, AND MILITARY ACCOUNTS
DEPARTMENT.

Appointments to the above Services are made by the Government of India, and no recruitment for them is undertaken by the Secretary of State for India. Except in the case of the Geological Survey, appointments to which are made on the advice of a Selection

Committee, the normal method of appointment to all these Services is by means of a competitive examination open to British subjects of Asiatic domicile. Enquiries may be addressed to the High Commissioner for India, India House, Aldwych, W.C.2.

INDIAN STATE RAILWAYS.

Recruitment for the Indian State Railways is no longer conducted by the Secretary of State. Appointments will in future be made by the Federal Railway Authority in India, and any recruitment undertaken in England will normally be conducted by the High Commis-

sioner for India. Vacancies for which the High Commissioner is likely to be required to recruit will occur only at irregular intervals, and applications will ordinarily be invited by advertisement in the Press.

MEMORANDUM ON RULES APPLICABLE TO OFFICERS OF THE SUPERIOR CIVIL SERVICES.

Full details regarding the scales of pay, leave, pension, etc., of Officers of the Superior Civil Services are given in the authorised text of the relevant rules for the time being in force. The rules in question are:—

For pay and passages The Superior Civil Services Rules.
For leave Fundamental Rules.
For pension Civil Service Regulations and the Superior Civil Services Rules.
The Premature Retirement Rules.
For commutation of pension. The Civil Pension (Commutation) Rules.
For Provident Funds. The I.C.S. Provident Fund Rules.
The I.C.S (non-European members) Provident Fund Rules.
The General Provident Fund (Superior Civil Services) Rules.

For Family Pension Funds.

The Superior Services (India Provident Fund) (Sterling accounts) Rules.
The Civil Engineers Provident Fund.

The I.C.S. Family Pension Fund Rules.
The I.C.S. Family Pension Fund (untransferred section) Provisional Rules.
The I.C.S. (non-European members) Family Pension Fund Rules.
The Superior Services (India) Family Pension Fund Rules.
The Superior Services (India) Family Pension Fund (Untransferred Section) Provisional Rules.

For the State Railway Provident Fund.

The State Railway Provident Fund Rules.
The Superior Services (Railway Provident Funds) (Sterling accounts) Rules.

APPOINTMENTS TO THE INDIAN SERVICES.
APPOINTMENTS TO THE INDIAN ARMY.

REGULATIONS FOR ADMISSION.

Candidates competing for commissions in the Indian Army are required to pass the Army Entrance Examination held by the Civil Service Commissioners. Forms of application for permission to attend the Examination may be obtained from the Secretary, Civil Service Commission, Burlington Gardens, W. 1. These examinations are held twice a year (in June and November) at various centres in the United Kingdom. Candidates must be between 18 and 19 years of age at a date specified in the notice of the examination, and must be of good health and character. The obligatory subjects are: Part I, English, General Knowledge, Interview, and Record and one of the following:—Modern Language, General History, Elementary Mathematics or Everyday Science. Candidates may also take not more than two of the following optional subjects: Part II,* Latin, Greek, French, German, Modern History, Lower Mathematics, Higher Mathematics, Physics plus Chemistry, Biology. In addition to these subjects candidates may be examined in Freehand or Geometrical Drawing. Further particulars regarding the examination are contained in the Regulations for admission to the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, which may be obtained from The U.S. of State, War Office, S.W.1. Question papers set at certain previous examinations may be purchased either directly from the following branches of H.M. Stationery Office: Kingsway, London, W.C.2; 120 George Street, Edinburgh; York Street, Manchester; 1, St. Andrew's Crescent, Cardiff; 80, Chichester Street, Belfast; or through any bookseller.

INDIAN ARMY CADETSHIPS AT SANDHURST.

The notice of the examination issued by the War Office specifies the number of cadetships offered at the examination which are tenable at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst. Of these the greater number are for the British Army, but 100 cadetships carrying appointment to the Indian Army are offered at Sandhurst in the course of a year. As the examinations are held twice a year, the number of these cadetships offered in connection with any examination is limited to 50, subject, however, to the requirements of the Indian Army at the time. Not more than 10 of these cadetships will be allotted at each examination to King's India Cadets nominated by the Secretary of State for India (see below) and one or two to King's Cadets nominated by the Army Council, the balance to be allotted by open competition. After the claims of King's Cadets and King's India Cadets have been met the remaining appointments will be made on the following principles:—

(a) The first consideration will be given to those cadets who pass out highest on the list of successful candidates from the Royal Military College, but the Secretary of State for India reserves the right to consider

special family claims, or a desire to serve in India, irrespective of a candidate's position on the list.

(b) A cadet having any special family claim on the Indian Army, or a desire to serve in India, should note this on the preference form which he will be required to fill in during his last term at the college. Considerable weight will always be given to the recommendations of the Military Secretary, India Office, and, where a cadet is on the Military Secretary's list and also has family connections with the Indian Services, two of the primary conditions of appointment to the Indian Army are fulfilled, but his chances of obtaining the appointment will be greatly enhanced if he has taken a high place in the final examination at the Royal Military College.

A limited number of Entrance Scholarships are awarded by the War Office at each Army Entrance Examination to candidates (other than King's Cadets and King's India Cadets), in order of merit in the examination who obtain not less than 60 per cent. of the maximum marks.

Under present conditions the course at Sandhurst lasts for 18 months. At the end of the period of training the Indian Army cadets who are recommended for commissions will be gazetted to the Unattached List for the Indian Army, and will be sent out to India for attachment to a British unit in that country for one year. On the conclusion of a year's service with a British unit, they will, if recommended, be posted to a unit of the Indian Army.

FEES.

The fees ordinarily payable by cadets are £60 a term for the son of an officer or soldier and £100 a term for the son of a private gentleman. These rates may, however, be reduced by 25 per cent., 50 per cent., 75 per cent., or 100 per cent., if the means of the parents or guardians justify such reduction. In addition to this fee a contribution of £55 will be required from the parent or guardian of each cadet in aid of uniform, books, and other incidentals for the first two terms, and £15 for the third term. In the event of a candidate being admitted to the College at a reduced rate of fees, the total contribution in aid of uniform, books, etc., will be reduced in the same proportion. The sums due should be paid to the College Accountant immediately the cadet enters the Royal Military College, except in the case of King's India Cadets, regarding whom information, as to fees, etc., payable and the authority to which payment should be made, will be furnished to the parent or guardian by the India Office. Any expenditure on uniform, books, and incidentals in excess of this £70 must be paid by the parent or guardian of the cadet at the end of the course.

While under instruction cadets are expected to be supplied with pocket money by their parents or guardians at the rate of £3 a month.

* No Candidate may offer similar subjects in Part I. and II.
† This number includes 10 Army Cadets.

INDIAN ARMY—continued.

Free issues of revolver, binoculars and prismatic compasses will be made to the cadets at the Royal Military College, and on being commissioned they will receive an outfit allowance of £50 and a free issue of Camp Kit.

In the case of all cadets a contribution is made by Government which is intended to cover the cost of messing, laundry, and servants at the Colleges, but any expense incurred beyond the amount of this Government contribution must be paid by the cadet or his parent.

KING'S INDIA CADETSHIPS.

Twenty King's India Cadetships at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, are offered every year. These King's India Cadetships are confined by statute to the sons of persons who have served the Crown in India. To be eligible for these cadetships, candidates must attain a qualifying standard at the Army Entrance Examination. The nominations are made by the Secretary of State for India according to definite rules, the length and distinction of the father's service being the primary consideration. Special consideration is, however, given to the sons of deceased officers. The services of relatives other than the father cannot be taken into consideration in allotting these cadetships.

The fees normally payable in respect of King's India Cadets will be at the rate of £30 a term, together with the payment of £35 for uniform, books, etc., covering the whole period of the course. In cases in which the Secretary of State for India considers that the pecuniary circumstances of the parent (or cadet) justify the concession, remission of fees and contribution, wholly or in part, may be granted.

A King's India Cadetship carries the right of appointment to the Indian Army.

Three Honorary King's India Cadetships at the Royal Military College are offered annually.

Such Cadets are appointed from—

  • (a) The sons of officers of the Indian Army who were killed in action, or who have died of wounds received in action within six months of such wounds having been received, or from illness brought on by fatigue, privation, or exposure incident to active operations in the field before an enemy, within six months after their having been first certified to be ill.
  • (b) The sons of officers of the Indian Army who have attained the brevet or substantive rank of Major or Lieutenant-Colonel, and have performed long or distinguished service.

Note.—The sons of officers of the Indian Medical Service, of Royal Artillery officers who have been selected for continuous service in India, and of Royal Engineer officers who have completed 20 years' service for Indian pension are eligible under the same conditions as the sons of officers of the Indian Army.

The age limits, times of nomination, and conditions of eligibility as regards examinations are the same as for King's India Cadetships.

An Honorary King's India Cadetship carries with it no pecuniary advantage.

Only those candidates who intend to apply for appointment to the Indian Army on being commissioned are eligible for nomination to King's India Cadets or Honorary King's India Cadets. In the case of a candidate who has been nominated to a King's India Cadetship and who withdraws his election for appointment to the Indian Army, or leaves the College of his own accord, or is removed or expelled under the rules of the College, before completing the course and being commissioned, the nomination will be cancelled, and full fees will be payable from the date of the cadet's admission to the College at the normal rates laid down in Art. 738 of the Royal Warrant for Pay, etc.

The normal fees will similarly be payable after appointment to the Unattached List, officer elects to remain in the British Army.

Forms of application for King's India Cadetships can be obtained from the Secretary, Military Department, India Office.

UNIVERSITY CANDIDATES.

A limited number of appointments to the Unattached List for the Indian Army made by the War Office from candidates nominated by the principal Universities in the United Kingdom. The Regulations for University Candidates can be obtained from the War Office.

TERRITORIAL FORCE AND SUPPLEMENTARY RESERVE.

A limited number of appointments are also offered to officers of the Territorial Army and Supplementary Reserve, between the ages of 21 and 25, who undertake the Final Examination at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, under arrangements made by The War Office.

PAY ON BEING COMMISSIONED.

Cadets from Sandhurst who are gazetted to the Unattached List for the Indian Army receive pay at the rate admissible to a Second Lieutenant of British Infantry, from the date of their commission to the date of their arrival in India. Ration allowance at current rate is also payable up to date of embarkation. On attachment to a British unit in India, Second Lieutenants of the Unattached List will receive pay at Rs. 385 per mensem, plus Rs. 20 per mensem, if free quarters are not provided. On appointment to an Indian regiment they will receive the Indian Army pay and allowances of their rank.

PAY, ETC., OF OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN ARMY. Information regarding the pay, leave, promotion and pensions of Indian Army Officers may be obtained from the Secretary, Military Department, India Office.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

All candidates appointed to permanent commissions in the Indian Army are required, as a condition of their appointment, to subscribe to the "Indian Military Widows' and Orphans' Fund." The regulations of this Fund can be obtained on application to the Controller of Pensions Funds, India Office. In addition to these pen-

INDIAN ARMY—continued.

sions, the families of officers of the Indian Army are eligible for the pensions laid down in the Royal Warrant for the pay of the British Army.

SCHOLARSHIPS.

Officers appointed to commissions on the Unattached List for the Indian Army, from the Royal Military College and the Universities, are eligible equally with officers appointed to the British Service for the scholarships of the annual

value of £20, tenable for three years, which will be awarded to young officers on first appointment to commissions for promise of special ability for regimental work, or for staff or other special work as laid down in Article 684 of the Royal Warrant for Pay, etc. (1931).

These conditions are subject to such alterations at any time as may be determined by the Secretary of State for India.

INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

METHOD OF APPOINTMENT.

The Indian Medical Service offers a permanent career with wide opportunities of medical experience including Clinical, Preventive, Specialist and Research work. At the beginning of his career an officer is employed on the military side, which has medical charge of the Indian Army. Promotion is on a time scale up to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel and by selection to the ranks of Colonel and Major-General. An officer may apply, after one year's Indian service, to have his name registered for transfer to the civil side, from which appointments are made to civil surgeries, which are established at the principal civil centres to provide for the medical needs of civil officials and for general medical administrative purposes; to specialist (e.g., Public Health and Bacteriological) Services; to research posts and to clinical appointments at the Medical Colleges.

Appointment to the Indian Medical Service is at present, and until further notice, made by nomination by the Secretary of State. Applications from candidates of pure European descent who are under thirty-two years of age and who are registered under the Medical Acts in force in Great Britain and Northern Ireland, should be addressed to the Secretary, Military Department, India Office, Whitehall, S.W.1. To assist him in making appointments the Secretary of State has constituted a Selection Committee for the purpose of summoning and interviewing such applicants as appear to be prima facie suitable and making recommendations for appointment.

A similar Committee has been set up in India to examine the qualifications of Indian and Anglo-Indian candidates for appointment. Applications from these candidates should be addressed to the Director-General, Indian Medical Service, Delhi (or Simla), India. They will then be considered by the Selection Committee in India, but, in order to obviate the necessity for the return to India for interview of candidates who are in Europe at the date of selection such candidates, if prima facie suitable, may, at the request of the authorities in India, be summoned to appear before an Interviewing Committee in London, who will report the result of the interview to the Selection Committee in India. Similarly, European candidates, should their place of residence make it more convenient, may be summoned to appear before an Interviewing Committee in India, who will report the result of the interview to the Secretary of State.

Successful candidates are normally appointed in the rank of Lieutenant.

MEETINGS OF SELECTION COMMITTEE.

The Selection Committee constituted by the Secretary of State normally meets at the India Office in April, July, October and December of each year.

ANTEDATES IN COMMISSION.

Candidates possessing certain higher medical qualifications or holding a diploma in Public Health or having served in certain hospital appointments may be granted an antedate in their commissions. Persons holding or about to hold resident posts at recognised hospitals may be seconded, for a period not exceeding twelve months, in those posts. The maximum period of antedate, or antedate and secondment combined, is limited to eighteen months. The period of antedate and/or secondment will count as commissioned service for the purposes of increments of pay, promotion, and retired pay, but not for gratuity on voluntary retirement.

INSTRUCTION PRIOR TO EMBARKATION.

Officers are required to undergo Courses of Instruction at the Royal Army Medical College, Millbank, and at Aldershot, lasting approximately three months, prior to their embarkation for India on first appointment.

OUTFIT ALLOWANCE.

Officers on appointment will receive an allowance of £75 towards the cost of outfit.

PASSAGES.

An officer on appointment is provided with a free passage to India. The families of officers who are married prior to the date of the officer's embarkation on first appointment will also be provided with free passage to India, subject to the payment of messing charges.

PRIVATE PRACTICE.

With the exception of administrative officers, military or civil, and officers holding certain special appointments, officers are not debarred from taking private practice so long as it does not interfere with their official duties.

OTHER CONDITIONS OF SERVICE.

Information regarding the pay, leave, promotion prospects and pensions and any further information regarding the conditions of service of I.M.S. officers may be obtained from the Secretary, Military Department, India Office.

ROYAL INDIAN NAVY.

The Royal Indian Navy, which was re-organised as a combatant force in 1928, at present consists of five sloops, a surveying ship, a Depot ship for training purposes, and various minor craft. The Executive Branch includes the Flag Officer Commanding and other officers in the ranks of Captain, Commander, Lieutenant-Commander, Lieutenant and Sub-Lieutenant, and the Engineering Branch consists of the Engineer-Captain and other officers in the ranks of Engineer-Commander, Engineer-Lieutenant and Engineer-Sub-Lieutenant. The Commissioned Officers of the Royal Indian Navy are mainly European but Indians have been admissible since 1928. The ships are manned by Indian ratings, although a number of European Warrant officers are also employed. The Royal Indian Navy is organised and equipped on lines similar to the Royal Navy. Commissioned officers attend at intervals

throughout their careers courses of training in this country under the Admiralty and are thus kept in touch with the practice in the Royal Navy.

The normal method of entry into the Royal Indian Navy is through the examination for Naval Cadetships (Special Entry) held by the Civil Service Commission in March, June and November of each year, the age limits for which are approximately 17 to 19 years. The number of executive and engineer vacancies declared for the Royal Indian Navy varies from time to time. All particulars regarding these examinations may be obtained on written application to the Secretary, Civil Service Commission, Burlington Gardens, S.W.1, whilst information regarding the conditions of appointment to, and service in, the Royal Indian Navy may be obtained from the Secretary, Military Department, India Office.

BENGAL PILOT SERVICE.

Appointments to the Bengal Pilot Service are made by the Government of India with the assistance of a Federal Public Service Commission. Recruitment in England, which is

conducted by the High Commissioner for India, is undertaken only when no suitable candidates are available in India.

PARLIAMENTARY PAPERS.

INDIA OFFICE LIBRARY.

The Library was founded in 1801 by the East India Company. It contains about 130,000 printed books, principally relating to the East, and 15,000 manuscripts and xylographs. An account of the history and contents of the Library is given in The India Office Library, by A. J. Arberry (London, 1938).

There are the following catalogues, and others are in preparation:—

ORIENTAL MANUSCRIPTS AND XYLOGRAPHS.IN PRINT.

Sanskrit.—Catalogue of the Sanskrit and Prākṛit manuscripts in the Library of the India Office. Vol. I. J. Eggeling. London, 1888-1904.

Vol. II. A. B. Keith and F. W. Thomas. Oxford, 1934.

(This catalogue includes descriptions of all the Sanskrit MSS belonging to the Special Collections, excepting those of the Royal Society Collection).

Catalogues of the Special Collections:—

„ Mackenzie Collection. H. H. Wilson. Calcutta, 1828, 2 vols.; 2nd. Ed. in one vol. Madras, 1882.

„ Burnell Collection (Vedic MSS.). A. C. Burnell. London, 1869.

„ Hodgson Collection. Sir W. W. Hunter. (Life of B. H. Hodgson, London, 1896, p. 344.)

„ Tagore Collection. S. Mohun Tagore. Calcutta, 1890.

„ „ T. Aufrecht. (Zeitsch. der Deutsch. Morgenländ. Gesellschaft, lviii, pp. 525-38, 1904.)

„ Böhler Collection. G. Böhler. (Zeitsch. der Deutsch. Morgenländ. Gesellschaft, xlii, pp. 530-52, 1888.)

„ Royal Society Collection. C. H. Tawney and F. W. Thomas. London, 1903.

„ Aufrecht Collection (MSS., Indices, Pratika-lists, &c.). F. W. Thomas. (Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society, London, 1908, pp. 1029-63.)

Arabic.—Vol. I. O. Loth. London, 1877. Vol. II, Pt. I (Qur'anic Literature). C. A. Storey, London, 1930. Pt. II. (Sūfism and Ethics). A. J. Arberry, London, 1936. Pt. III. (Fiqh), R. Levy, London, 1937. Part IV. R. Levy (in the Press).

Arabic and Persian.—Royal Society Collection. E. Denison Ross and E. G. Browne. London, 1902.

Persian.—H. Ethé. Vol. I. Oxford, 1903. Vol. II. Index, etc., revised and completed by E. Edwards. Oxford, 1936.

Bengali and Assamese.—J. F. Blumhardt. Oxford, 1924.

Chinese.—Rev. J. Summers. London, 1872.

„ (Tripitaka). S. Beal. Devonport, 1876.

„ Bunyiu Nanjio. Oxford, 1883.

Hindustani.—J. F. Blumhardt. Oxford, 1926.

Marathi.—J. F. Blumhardt and S. G. Kanhere (in the Press).

Javanese.—S. Keyser. (Bijdragen tot de Taal-, Land- en Volkenkunde van Neerlandsch Indië. The Hague, 1854, part II, pp. 339-42.)

Malay.—H. van der Tuuk. (Tijdschr. voor Nederlandsch Indië, 1849 (1), pp. 385-400.)

Oriya.—J. F. Blumhardt. Oxford, 1924.

Pali.—H. Oldenberg. (Journ. Pali Text Soc., London, 1882, pp. 59-128.)

„ (Mandalay).—V. Fausböll. (Journ. Pali Text Soc., Woking, 1897, pp. 1-52.)

Zend and Pahlavi.—M. N. Dhalla (Iranian MSS. in the Library of the India Office, Journal of the R. Asiatic Society, 1912, pp. 387-98).

IN MANUSCRIPT.

Sanskrit.—Mackenzie Collections (R. Rost: Burmese (Mandalay), Taw Sein Ko (Notes). incomplete).

Arabic.—Delhi Collection. Siamese.—H. Alabaster.

Persian.—Delhi Collection. Batta.—H. van der Tuuk.

Ethiopic and Syriac.—W. Wright. Tibetan.—L. de la Vallée Poussin.

Chinese.—S. Beal. Hindi.—J. F. Blumhardt.

Chinese.—Royal Society Collection.—H. A. Giles. Gujarati.—J. F. Blumhardt.

Burmese.—E. Chevillion. Panjabi, Pushta and Sindhi.—J. F. Blumhardt.

Malayalam.—C. A. Menon.

EUROPEAN MANUSCRIPTS.

Catalogue of MSS. in European Languages.—

Vol. I. The Mackenzie Collections. Pt. I: The 1822 Collection and the Private Collection. C. O. Blagden, London, 1916 Pt. II. The General Collection. J. Charpentier and E. H. Johnston (in the Press).

Vol. II, Pt. I: The Orme Collection. S. C. Hill, London, 1916. Pt. II. Minor Collections and Miscellaneous MSS. G. R. Kaye and E. H. Johnston. Section I. (Nos. 1-538). G. R. Kaye. London, 1936. Section II. (Nos. 539-842). E. H. Johnston (in the Press).

PRINTED BOOKS AND LITHOGRAPHS.

" Vol. I, European Books. London, 1888.

" Supplement. London, 1895.

" Supplement 2: 1895-1908. London, 1909.

" Accessions: 1. 1911 (1); 2. 1911 (2); 3. 1912 (1); 4. 1912 (2); 5. 1913 (1); 6. 1913 (2);
7. 1918; 8. 1919; 9. 1920; 10. 1921; 11. 1923; 12. 1924; 13. 1926; 14. 1928
15. 1931; 16. 1934; 17. 1936; 18. 1936.

(A revised catalogue of European Books is in preparation.)

" Vol. II, Pt. 1. Sanskrit Books. R. Rost. London, 1897. Revised edition to 1932.
Pran Nath and J. B. Chaudhuri. Section I. (A-G). London, 1938.

" Pt. 2. Hindustani Books. J. F. Blumhardt. London, 1900.

" Pt. 3. Hindi, Panjabi, Pushtu, and Sindhi Books. J. F. Blumhardt. Lond., 1902.

" Pt. 5. Marathi and Gujarati Books. J. F. Blumhardt. London, 1907.

" Pt. 6. Persian Books. A. J. Arberry. London, 1937.

(Others in manuscript.)

EXTRACT FROM THE RULES FOR THE USE OF THE LIBRARY.
  1. 1. The use of the Library (including the Reading Room) is restricted to (a) members of the establishments of the India Office and of the Office of the High Commissioner for India, (b) persons named in the India Office List, (c) gazetted officers of the Indian Army or of the British Army in India, and (d) such other persons as have obtained tickets for that purpose.
  2. 2. Subject to the following provisions, volumes may be taken away on loan:—(a) The Librarian shall require from each person a written acknowledgment for every volume borrowed. (b) No person shall have on loan at one time more than eight volumes. But the Librarian may exempt scholars engaged in special researches from this limitation as well as from the provisions of section (d) below. (c) Manuscripts, original drawings, bound volumes of tracts, rare publications and works of reference shall not be lent to individuals. But the Librarian shall have power to authorise such loans in exceptional circumstances. If such a loan is made, the Librarian may require the borrower to sign a bond. (d) Books may be borrowed for one month only in the first instance, but this period may on written application be extended by five successive renewals, so that the total duration of the loan shall not exceed six months, on the expiry of which period the volumes must be returned to the Library; thereafter, if the books are not required by another reader, they may be issued as a fresh loan. A current periodical may be retained for not more than one week. (e) Any volume may at any time be recalled at the discretion of the Librarian. (f) A borrower shall not pass into the possession of another person any volume lent to him. (g) Borrowed volumes may not be taken abroad except by the special leave of the Librarian.
  3. 3. The hours during which the Library is open shall be from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m., except on Saturdays, when it shall close at 1 p.m. It shall be closed on Sundays, Good Friday and the day following, Christmas Day, the Saturday before Whitsun, and the Bank Holidays; also during the first two weeks in September for cleaning purposes.
  4. 4. No book or manuscript will be issued later than half-an-hour before the time of closing.

OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY OF STATE
FOR BURMA,

WHITEHALL, S.W.1.

THE MOST HON. THE MARQUESS OF ZETLAND, P.C., G.C.S.I., G.C.I.E.

CLERICAL DIVISION

Higher Clerical Officer—W. H. Ford.

J. P. Quilley, V. R. B. Shalson.

HIGH COMMISSIONER.

Section 150 of the Government of Burma Act, 1935, provides that there may be a High Commissioner for Burma in the United Kingdom if the Governor of Burma in his discretion thinks fit so to direct. At present, however, by agreement with the Government of India, the Government of Burma continue to make use of the services of the High Commissioner for India as their agent in London except as regards trade matters.

As regards trade matters, their agent is the Director of the Imperial Institute, S.W.7, Sir H. A. F. Lindsay, K.C.I.E., C.B.E. An exception to this arrangement is that the Timber Adviser to the High Commissioner for India, Sir H. W. A. Watson, acts for the Government of Burma in collaboration with the Imperial Institute in matters affecting timber.

GOVERNMENT OF BURMA.

Headquarters of Government—Rangoon (cold weather), Maymyo (hot weather).

Governor—His Excellency the Hon. SIR ARCHIBALD DOUGLAS COCHRANE, G.C.M.G.
K.C.S.I., D.S.O.

BURMA CIVIL SERVICE (CLASS I).

The Burma Civil Service (Class I.) is the Service in Burma corresponding to the Indian Civil Service in India, and appointments to this Service are made by the Secretary of State for Burma. There are at present three methods by which appointments are made to the Burma Civil Service (Class I.) for which men only are eligible, namely,

  1. (1) By written competitive examination held in England;
  2. (2) By selection in England;
  3. (3) By written competitive examination held in Burma.

The written competitive examination is the same as that which takes place annually for appointment to the Indian Civil Service and the Administrative Class of the Home Civil Service, and is held at the same time. A candidate, on payment of a single fee, may enter for all or any of these Services at the same examination. Candidates must be between the ages of 21 and 24 on the 1st August of the year in which the examination is held, and must be British subjects either of European descent, or of Burman domicile or descent.

The qualifications for appointment by selection are identical with those required for the Indian Civil Service (see page 154). The selection of candidates is made by the same board as that which selects candidates for the Indian Civil Service, and a candidate may indicate on his application form that he also wishes to be considered for the Indian Civil Service.

Candidates for the written competitive examination held in Burma are required to be British subjects domiciled in Burma, to be between the ages of 21 and 24 on the 1st January of the year in which the examination is held and to hold a Degree of an approved University or the Senior Diploma of the Mayo College, Ajmer.

The terms of the probation of successful candidates are similar to those of the Indian Civil Service. The work and conditions of service generally of the Burma Civil Service (Class I.) are also the same as those of the Indian Civil Service (see memorandum on that Service on page 154), but in addition a Burma allowance is admissible at the following rates:—

Pay Amount of
Burma Allowance
admissible.
Rs. per mensem. Rs. per mensem.
200 to 299 .. .. 50
300 to 399 .. .. 60
400 to 499 .. .. 75
500 to 599 .. .. 90
600 to 799 .. .. 105
800 to 999 .. .. 120
1,000 to 1,499 .. .. 135
1,500 to 1,999 .. .. 150
2,000 and upwards .. .. 165

Further information and forms of application for appointment by competitive examination or selection in England may be obtained from the Under-Secretary of State, Burma Office, Whitehall, S.W.1.

Enquiries regarding the competitive examination in Burma should be addressed to the Secretary to the Government of Burma, Home Department.

CANDIDATE SELECTED AFTER OPEN COMPETITION HELD IN LONDON IN JULY, 1938.

M. A. Maybury.

CANDIDATE SELECTED ON THE RECOMMENDATION OF A SELECTION BOARD WHICH MET IN JULY, 1938.

R. G. Rennie.

CANDIDATES SELECTED AFTER OPEN COMPETITION IN RANGOON, 1938.

Maung San Lin.

W. J. Carrott.

O. H. Molloy.

BURMA POLICE (CLASS I.)

The Burma Police (Class I.) is the Service in Burma corresponding to the Indian Police in India, and appointments to this Service are made by the Secretary of State for Burma.

The method of entry is by a written competitive examination held concurrently with the Indian Police and the Army and Air Force entrance examinations.

The subjects of the examination, the conditions of eligibility, the fee payable and the method of application are the same as those laid down for the Indian Police. Payment of one fee will admit candidates to the examination for the Indian Police as well as for the Burma Police (Class I.).

Successful candidates are appointed on probation and attached to the Police Training School at Mandalay. In all other respects the

conditions of their probation are identical with those of the Indian Police.

The work and conditions of service generally of the Burma Police (Class I.) are also the same as for the Indian Police (see the memorandum on that Service on page 157), but in addition, a Burma allowance is admissible at the rates given in the memorandum on the Burma Civil Service (Class I.) on page 191. Further information and forms of application may be obtained from the Under-Secretary of State, Burma Office, Whitehall, S.W.I.

Entry into the Burma Police in Burma is confined to candidates domiciled in Burma. Forms of application, together with Rules, Regulations, and the syllabus of the examination may be obtained from the Secretary to the Government of Burma, Home Department.

THE BURMA MILITARY POLICE.

The Burma Military Police consists of the three battalions which before the separation of Burma from India were the garrison battalions of the Burma Military Police. They are, in essence, a civil force and are now under the

control of the Home Minister. Officers for the Burma Military Police are found by secondment for short tours of duty from British Service and Indian Army Units.

THE BURMA FRONTIER SERVICE.

The Burma Frontier Service is a service which is classed as "Superior," and is, as its name implies, a service whose members are appointed to posts in certain Frontier districts of Burma, notably in the Shan States, the Chin Hills and the Myitkyina District.

Officers are appointed, and their conditions of service regulated, by the Governor of Burma in his discretion. European officers are recruited by the Secretary of State on behalf of the Governor. Applications are invited by advertisement in the general Press as vacancies arise. Officers of Asiatic domicile are recruited locally from officers serving in the Burma Civil Service (Class II.).

A European candidate must be unmarried and between the ages of 21 and 25. He must be of sound bodily health, and must produce evidence of having received a good general education. The possession of a University degree is regarded as an additional qualification. A candidate's ability to ride is also taken into consideration. He must be prepared to serve for long periods in remote parts of Burma where there will be little society.

Candidates are required to attend, at their own expense, at the Burma Office, for a personal interview. Those selected are required to produce satisfactory evidence of their date of birth, and to appear before the Medical Board at the Burma Office for examination as to their physical fitness for appointment. There is no charge for this examination, but candidates must be prepared to pay their own travelling expenses.

Successful European candidates must serve for two years on probation, and remain unmarried for this period, under penalty of forfeiture of their appointment. An officer is not confirmed in his appointment until he

  1. (a) has satisfactorily completed such course of practical training in the district as may from time to time be prescribed;
  2. (b) has satisfactorily passed such departmental examinations as may from time to time be prescribed;
  3. (c) is considered by the Government to be fit for confirmation.

If an officer fails to be confirmed within a period of three years from the date of his appointment his services may be dispensed with summarily.

THE BURMA FRONTIER SERVICE—continued.PAY.

An officer's salary rises from £450 in his first year of service to £1,485 in his 23rd year. These figures include overseas pay, and are calculated on the exchange value of the rupee being rs. 6d. The pay of the Service is not subject to the vote of the Burma Legislature.

LEAVE.

An officer earns for every year spent on duty rather more than 2\frac{1}{2} months leave on full pay, or five months on half-pay. He cannot, of course, take leave every year, but he can accumulate his leave.

PASSAGES

On proceeding to take up his appointment a European officer is provided with a free first-class passage to Burma, subject to the condition that its cost shall be refunded in the event of an officer resigning before the expiry of the period of probation on grounds other than ill-health, certified to the satisfaction of the Government. If on the expiry of the period of probation he is not confirmed in his appointment, or if he is compelled by certified ill-health to resign the service before such expiry, he will be provided with a similar free passage back to England, or at his option to any other country at a cost not exceeding that of such passage.

Every European officer after his period of probation is entitled to four free first-class passages to and from Burma, and a number not exceeding four for his wife, the number being calculated according to the officer's length of service at the date of his marriage. If he prefers to do so, he can travel at a cheaper rate, and so increase the number of times he is able to come home at Government expense.

PENSION.

The pension which an officer can earn is fixed according to a scale which rises from Rs.6,000 a year after 25 years' qualifying service to Rs.7,000 after 30 years or more.

PROVIDENT FUND.

Every officer contributes to a Provident Fund, and on retirement his contributions are repaid to him in a lump sum, to which compound interest is added.

Further information about the Burma Frontier Service may be obtained from the Under-Secretary of State, Burma Office, Whitehall, S.W.I.

BURMA ECCLESIASTICAL ESTABLISHMENT,
(CHURCH OF INDIA, BURMA AND CEYLON)

1. Appointments of chaplains to the Burma Ecclesiastical Establishment are made by nomination by the Secretary of State for Burma on the recommendation of the India and Burma Chaplaincies Board.

2. Candidates for these appointments, which are made as vacancies occur, must be Priests who have been at least three years in Holy Orders, and should ordinarily be between the ages of twenty-seven and thirty-four years.

3. Applications for nominations should be submitted to the India and Burma Chaplaincies

Board of the Church of England. A memorandum giving the conditions of service together with forms of application may be obtained from the Under-Secretary of State, Burma Office, Whitehall, S.W.1.

4. An Ecclesiastical Establishment is maintained in India as well as in Burma, but not in Ceylon, and a candidate may indicate on his application form if he also desires to be considered for appointment to the Indian Ecclesiastical Establishment.

BURMA RAILWAY SERVICES.

The Burma Railway Services are under the control of the Burma Railway Board, by whom appointments are made. Any recruitment of candidates undertaken in this country on behalf of the Board is conducted by the High Commissioner for India, India House, Aldwych,

London, W.C.2, to whose office enquiries should be addressed. Vacancies for which the High Commissioner is likely to be required to recruit occur only at irregular intervals and applications are ordinarily invited by advertisement in the press.

AGRICULTURAL, EDUCATIONAL, FOREST, ENGINEERING
AND VETERINARY SERVICES IN BURMA.

Recruitment for appointments to the above-mentioned service posts in Burma is not ordinarily made by the Secretary of State for Burma. Such appointments are now under the control of the Government of Burma and any recruitment undertaken for them in this country is conducted by the High Commissioner for India, India House, Aldwych, London, W.C.2. These appointments occur only at

irregular intervals. Applications for appointments for which the High Commissioner may be asked by the Government of Burma to recruit are ordinarily invited by advertisement in the general press. Particulars as to such Rules as may have been issued by the Government of Burma regulating these Services may be obtained from the High Commissioner for India.

GEOLOGICAL SURVEY, BURMA CUSTOMS SERVICE,
BURMA AUDIT DEPARTMENT.

Recruitment to these Services is normally undertaken in Burma. Enquiries concerning entry to the above-mentioned Departments

should be addressed to the High Commissioner for India, India House, Aldwych, London, W.C.2.

BURMA DEFENCE FORCE.

The Burma Defence Force is under the control of the Governor of Burma and consists of four parts:—

(a) The Burma Army (i.e., forces subject to the Burma Army Act) comprising the four existing battalions of the Burma Rifles which are shortly to be raised to five.

(b) The Imperial Forces in Burma, viz.:—
2 British Infantry battalions,
1 Indian Mountain Battery R.A.
1 Indian Company Sappers and Miners.

(c) The Burma Auxiliary Force and the Burma Territorial Force.

All the above, which form the Army in Burma, are under the immediate command of the General Officer Commanding who is

responsible to the Governor. The Army Headquarters are at Maymyo.

(d) The Burma Frontier Force, comprising five battalions which, before the separation of Burma from India, were the frontier battalions of the Burma Military Police. The Force is under the immediate command of an Inspector-General who in turn is responsible to the Governor. The Headquarters of the Force are at Rangoon.

The principal peace-time duties of the Force are watch and ward of the frontier, and the maintenance of order in the rough tribal areas in which its units are chiefly stationed.

Officers for the Burma Defence Force are found by secondment for short tours of duty from British Service and Indian Army Units.

MEDICAL SERVICES IN BURMA.

The Medical needs of the Army and of the European members of the Services in Burma

continue to be met by the secondment of officers from the Indian Medical Service.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN BURMA.

GEORGE R. I.

GEORGE THE SIXTH, by the Grace of God, of Great Britain, Ireland and the British Dominions beyond the Seas, King, Defender of the Faith, Emperor of India.

To all to whom these presents shall come :—

Whereas it hath been represented unto Us that it is advisable that the rank and precedence of persons holding appointments in Burma should be regulated by Royal Warrant, We do therefore hereby declare that it is Our will and pleasure that the following table be henceforth observed with respect to the rank and precedence of persons hereinafter named, namely :—

  1. 1. Governor of Burma.
  2. 2. Chief Justice.
  3. *3. Counsellors ; Ministers.
  4. 4. Commander-in-Chief of His Majesty's Naval Forces in the East Indies.
  5. 5. General Officer Commanding.
  6. 6. President of the Senate.
  7. 7. Speaker of the House of Representatives.
  8. 8. Financial Adviser ; Puisne Judges.
  9. 9. Auditor-General ; Chairman, Public Service Commission.
  10. 10. Chief Commissioner of Railways ; Financial Commissioners.
  11. 11. Advocate-General ; Vice-Chancellor, University of Rangoon.
  12. 12. Commissioners of Divisions within their respective charges.
  13. 13. Members of the Burma Civil Service (Class I) of 30 years' standing, whose position, but for this article, would not be lower than Article 15 ; Officer Commanding, Rangoon Brigade, within his area.
  14. 14. Brigadiers ; Inspector-General, Frontier Force ; Inspector-General of Police.
  15. 15. Chief Secretary ; Commissioners of Divisions ; Secretary to the Governor.
  16. †16. Chairman of the Port Commissioners, Rangoon ; Chairman of the Rangoon Development Trust ; Mayor of Rangoon (within Rangoon) ; Secretaries to Government.
  17. 17. Accountant-General ; Additional and Joint Secretaries to Government ; Chief Commercial Superintendent, Burma Railways ; Chief Conservator of Forests ; Chief Engineers, including the Chief Engineer, Burma Railways ; Chief Medical Officer, Burma Railways ; Chief Transportation Officer, Burma Railways ; Colonels ; Controller of Railway Accounts ; Director of Agriculture ; Director-General of Posts and Telegraphs ; Director of Public Health ; Director of Public Instruction ; Inspector-General of Civil Hospitals ; Inspector-General of Prisons ; Locomotive and Carriage Superintendent, Burma Railways ; Nautical Adviser and Principal Officer, Mercantile Marine Department.
  18. 18. Commissioner of Police, Rangoon ; Municipal Commissioner, Rangoon (within Rangoon).
  19. 19. Collector, Rangoon ; Deputy Commissioners of Districts ; District Magistrate, Rangoon ; Superintendents in the Shan States (within their respective charges.)
  20. 20. Air Adviser to the Government of Burma ; Chief Inspector of Factories, Explosives and Mines ; Collector of Customs ; Commissioner of Excise ; Commissioner of Income-Tax ; Commissioner of Settlements and Land Records ; Deputy Inspectors-General of Police ; Deputy Secretaries to Government ; Director, Defence Bureau ; Director, Meteorological Department ; Director, Veterinary Services ; District and Sessions Judges—within their respective charges ; members of the Public Service Commission ; Registrar, Co-operative Societies ; Registrar, High Court ; Superintendent, Archaeological Survey ; Superintending Geologist, Geological Department.
  21. 21. Chief Auditor, Railway Audit Department ; Chief Education Officer, Federated Shan States ; Chief Public Works Officer, Federated Shan States (within the Federated Shan States) ; Chief-Tele-communication Engineer ; Comptroller of Posts and Telegraphs Accounts ; Conservators of Forests ; Controller of

* NOTE.—The Premier ranks in this article senior to the Counsellors and other Ministers, and the Counsellors rank senior to Ministers other than the Premier.

† NOTE.—The Mayor of Rangoon ranks in this article senior to the Chairman of the Port Commissioners, Rangoon, and the Chairman of the Rangoon Development Trust.

Military Accounts ; Deputy Railway Commissioner and officers of similar status.* Deputy Director-General, Posts and Telegraphs ; Examiner, Local Fund Accounts ; Lieutenant-Colonels ; Members of the Burma Civil Service (Class I) of 18 years' standing, whose position, but for this article, would not be lower than Article 27 ; Principal Forest Officer, Federated Shan States, within the Federated Shan States ; Senior Chaplains of and above 20 years' Government service ; Superintending Engineers.†

22. Chief Judge of the Small Cause Court, Rangoon.

23. Administrator-General ; Government Inspector of Railways.

24. Deputy Controller of Railway Accounts ; Military Secretary to the Governor ; Secretaries to the Financial Commissioners.

25. Senior Chaplains other than those already specified.

26. Collector, Rangoon ; Deputy Commissioners of Districts ; District and Sessions Judges ; District Magistrate, Rangoon ; Principal Engineer and Ship Surveyor ; Settlement Officers ; Superintendents in the Shan States ; Superintendents of Police, within their own charges.

27. Government Advocates ; Officers of Class I Services, including the Class I Railway Services and the Burma Audit and Accounts Service Class I, and Senior Accounts Officers of the Burma Railways (of 20 years' standing or graded above officers of that standing) ; Port Officers, within their respective charges, and officers of similar status ; Principal, University College, Rangoon ; Secretaries to the Legislature ; Senior Inspectors of Mines ; Superintendents of the Survey of India.

28. Majors ; Members of the Burma Civil Service (Class I) of 12 years' standing ; Superintendents of Police and Deputy Commissioner of Police, Rangoon, of more than 15, but less than 20 years' standing ; Under Secretaries to Government.

29. Assistant Commissioners of Income-tax ; Assistant Superintendents of the Survey of India ; Officers of Class I Services including the Class I Railway Services and the Burma Audit and Accounts Service Class I, and Senior Accounts Officers of the Burma Railways (of 10 years' standing in the service or graded above officers of that standing) ; Superintendents of Police and the Deputy Commissioner of Police, Rangoon, of less than 15 years' standing ; Supervisor of Jail Industries.

30. Assistant Directors of Public Health ; Assistant Directors of Public Instruction ; Assistant Secretaries to Government ; Chemical Examiner ; Chief Inspector of Boilers ; Civil Surgeons not belonging to the Indian Medical Service ; Deputy Registrars of Co-operative Societies ; Divisional Engineers, Telegraphs ; Divisional Forest Officers ; Executive Engineers ; Inspectors of Factories, Senior Grade ; Inspectors of Mines ; Judges of the Small Cause Court, Rangoon ; Officers of Class II Services and Assistant Accounts Officers drawing the maximum pay of the time-scale or upwards ; Officers of District rank, Burma Railways ; Postmaster, Rangoon —within Rangoon ; Public Prosecutors ; Superintendent, Government Printing and Stationery ; Superintendents of Excise ; Superintendents of Central Jails not belonging to the Indian Medical Service.

1. The entries in the above table, which are in alphabetical order in each article, apply exclusively to the persons entered therein, and while regulating their relative precedence with each other do not give them any precedence over members of the non-official community resident on Burma, who shall take their place according to usage.

2. Officers in the above table will take precedence in order of the numbers of the entries. Those included in one number will take precedence inter se according to the date of entry into that number with the exception of officers of the Defence Services, who rank inter se in accordance with their seniority.

3. When an officer holds more than one position in the table, he will be entitled to the highest position accorded to him.

4. Officers who are temporarily officiating in any number in the table, will rank in that number below permanent incumbents.

5. All officers not mentioned in the above table, whose rank is regulated by comparison with rank in the army, will have the same rank with reference to civil servants as is enjoyed by military officers of equal grades.

6. An officer serving under the Government of India whose services are placed by that Government at the disposal of the Government of Burma, will, if his rank in the Indian Warrant of Precedence is relatively higher than the rank assigned in the above table to the appointment held by him in Burma, take rank in whatever article in the above table most nearly corresponds to the article in which he is ranked in India.

7. All other persons who may not be mentioned in this table will take rank according to general usage, which is to be explained and determined by the Governor in his discretion in case any question shall arise. When the position of any such person is so determined and notified, it shall be entered in the table in italics, provided he holds an appointment in Burma.

* NOTE.—Officers of similar status are :—Deputy Locomotive Superintendent, Deputy Carriage and Wagon Superintendent, Superintendent of Stores, Deputy Chief Engineer, Deputy Traffic Managers, Commercial Research Officer, and Statistical and Organisation Officer.

† NOTE.—Architectural, Electrical and Sanitary Specialist Officers will take precedence in accordance with the rank in the Public Works Department fixed for their appointments but junior to all Public Works Department officers of the corresponding rank.

‡ NOTE.—The Nautical Surveyor and Assistant Sea Transport Officer is an officer of similar status.

8. The following will take courtesy rank, as shown :—

Bishop of Rangoon.—Immediately after Article 9.

Consuls-General.—Immediately after Article 14, which includes Brigadiers.

Consuls.—Immediately after Article 17, which includes Colonels.

Vice-Consuls.—Immediately after Article 28, which includes Majors.

Consular officers de carrière will in their respective grades take precedence of consular officers who are not de carrière.

Among themselves Consular Officers will take precedence in their respective grades according to the dates of the Government of Burma notifications announcing the recognition of their appointments. An officiating incumbent of a grade will rank as an officer of that grade immediately below its permanent incumbents except that when an officer below the substantive grade of Consul officiates as a Consul-General he will be ranked with Consuls and assigned a place immediately after permanent Consuls.

9. The following may be given, by courtesy, precedence as shown below, provided that they do not hold appointments in Burma :—

Peers according to their precedence in England.

Knights of the Garter, the Thistle and St. Patrick.

Privy Councillors.

Advisers to the Secretary of State for Burma.

Baronets of England, Scotland, Ireland and the United

Kingdom according to date of Patents.

Knights Grand Cross of the Bath.

Knights Grand Commander of the Star of India.

Knights Grand Cross of St. Michael and St. George.

Knights Grand Commander of the Indian Empire.

Knights Grand Cross of the Royal Victorian Order.

Knights Grand Cross of the Order of the British Empire.

Knights Commander of the Bath.

Knights Commander of the Star of India.

Knights Commander of St. Michael and St. George.

Knights Commander of the Indian Empire.

Knights Commander of the Royal Victorian Order.

Knights Commander of the Order of the British Empire.

Knights Bachelor.

Immediately after Ministers and
Councillors, Article 3.

Immediately after Puisne Judges,
Article 8.

Immediately after Commissioners
within their charges, Article 12.

10. All ladies, unless by virtue of holding an appointment themselves they are entitled to a higher position in the table, will take place according to the rank herein assigned to their respective husbands, with the exception of wives and Peers and of ladies having precedence in England independently of their husbands, and who are not in rank below the daughters of Baronets; such ladies to take place according to their several ranks, with reference to such precedence in England, immediately after the wives of Ministers and Councillors.

GIVEN at Our Court of St. James's this First day of December in the year of Our Lord One Thousand nine hundred and thirty-eight and in the Second year of Our Reign.

BY HIS MAJESTY'S COMMAND,

ZETI-LAND.

ALPHABETICAL HONOURS LIST.

(a) This list includes the names of :

  1. (1) all persons appointed to the Orders of the Star of India and Indian Empire and to the Imperial Order of the Crown of India ;
  2. (2) Knights Bachelor created on account of services in India or Burma ;
  3. (3) Civil officials and non-officials of India and Burma appointed to other than Indian Orders ; and
  4. (4) Military Officers of the Indian and Burma Services awarded a Knighthood in other than Indian Orders.

Wood, F., O.B.E., 3 June, 1925.

Wood, W. G., C.S.I., 22 June, 1914.

C.I.E., 3 June 1899.

1 Jan., 1917 ; K.C.I.E., 16 Dec., 1904 ; K-i-H.

29 Oct., 1915 ; K.C.I.E., 1 Jan., 1913 ; C.B.,
29 Nov., 1900.

1937.
Zetland, the Most Hon. Marquess of, P.C., 1922 ;
G.C.S.I., 20 April, 1932 ; G.C.I.E., 21 Feb., 1917
Zia-ud-din, Ahmed Sir, Kt., 1 Jan., 1938 ; C.I.E.
3 June, 1915.
Zobel, Mrs. E., M.B.E., 30 Dec., 1919.
Zutahi, Rai Bahadur Pandit Brijmohan Nath, O.B.E.,
1 Jan., 1923.

RECORD OF SERVICES.

The following List includes Officers—

(1) of the Establishments of :—

  • (a) the Secretary of State for India,
  • (b) the Secretary of State for Burma,
  • (c) the High Commissioner for India,
  • (d) the Auditor of Indian Home Accounts ;

(2) of the

  • (a) All-India Services,
  • (b) Indian Political Service,
  • (c) Indian Ecclesiastical Establishment,
  • (d) Burma Class I Services,
  • (e) Burma Ecclesiastical Establishment ;

(3) in other Civil employment in India or in Burma whose substantive pay (exclusive of overseas pay) is at least Rs.1000 a month ;

(4) of the above classes who have retired since January, 1929, in addition to a few officers who retired before 1929. It has not been found practicable to include more than a limited number of officers who retired before that year.

NOTE.—Particulars of officiating appointments to posts other than those shewn in pp. 97-127 and 186-187 have not been recorded unless they appeared in the 1937 edition of the India Office List.

EXPLANATION OF ABBREVIATIONS.

Acad.—Academy.
Acadl.—Academical.
Acctnt.—Accountant.
Accts.—Accounts.
Addl.—Additional.
Addn.—Addition.
A.G.G.—Agent to Governor General.
Agric.—Agriculture.
Agricl.—Agricultural.
Appr.—Apprentice.
Apptd.—Appointed.
A.R.C.A.—Associate of the Royal College of Art, South Kensington.
A.T.C.L.—Associate of Trinity Coll., London.
Asst.—Assistant.
Att.—Attached.
Barr.—Barrister-at-Law.
Cantonmt.—Cantonment.
Capt.—Captain.
Cmd.—Command.
Col.—Colonel.
Coll.—College, Collegiate.
Collr.—Collector.
Commn.—Commission.
Commr.—Commissioner.
Comptr.—Comptroller.
Conf.—Confirmed.
Contr.—Controller.
Consvr.—Conservator.
Cov.—Covenanted.
Corpn.—Corporation.
D.A.G.—Deputy Accountant General.
Deleg.—Delegation.
Dep.—Deputy.
Depd.—Deputed.
Depn.—Deputation.
Dept.—Department.
Deptl.—Departmental.
Dir.—Director.
Divl.—Divisional.
Divn.—Division.
D.M.O.—District Medical Officer.
E.B. Rly.—Eastern Bengal Railway.
Ecclesl.—Ecclesiastical.
Edn.—Edition.
Educ.—Educated.
Educl.—Educational.
Educn.—Education.
E.I. Rly.—East Indian Railway.
Engr.—Engineer.
Engrg.—Engineering.
Establ.—Establishment.
Exam.—Examination.
Examr.—Examiner.
Exec.—Executive.
Exhibn.—Exhibition.
Exhibr.—Exhibitioner.
Expedn.—Expedition.
Expedny.—Expeditionary.
F.C.H.—Fellow of Cooper's Hill College.
Fin.—Finance.
Finl.—Financial.
For.—Foreign.
F.S.S. { Federated Shan States
{ Fellow of the Royal Statistical Society.
Gaz.—Gazetteer.
Gen.—General.
G.I.P. Rly.—Great Indian Peninsula Railway.
Govr.—Governor.
Govt.—Government.
Gr.—Grammar.
Hon.—Honorary.
Hosp.—Hospital.
I.A.R.O.—Indian Army Reserve of Officers.
I.A.S.—Indian Agricultural Service.
I.C.S.—Indian Civil Service.
I.E.S.—Indian Educational Service.
I.F.S.—Indian Forest Service.
Inspn.—Inspection.
Insp.—Inspector.
Inst.—Institute.
Instn.—Institution.
Instrn.—Instruction.
I.P.—Indian Police.
I.P.S.—India Police Service.
Irrig.—Irrigation.
I.S.E.—Indian Service of Engineers.
I.V.S.—Indian Veterinary Service.
Jud.—Judicial.
Junr.—Junior.
K.H.P.—King's Hon. Physician.
K.H.S.—King's Hon. Surgeon.
K.-i-H. medal.—Kaisar-i-Hind medal for public service in India.
Legisl.—Legislative.
Lieut.—Lieutenant.
Litho.—Lithographic.
Mag.—Magistrate.
Mechl.—Mechanical.
Med.—Medical.
Memo.—Memorandum.
Mil.—Military.
N.W.F.P.—North West Frontier Province.
N.W. Rly.—North Western Railway.
Offd.—Officiated.
Offg.—Officiating.
Offr.—Officer.
Optg.—Operating.
Parly.—Parliamentary.
Photo.—Photographic.
P.M.O.—Principal Medical Officer.
Pol.—Political.
Postmr.—Postmaster.
Prest.—President.
Presdy.—Presidency.
Probr.—Probationer.
Proby.—Probationary.
Profr.—Professor.
Prov.—Provinces.
Provl.—Provincial.
Publd.—Published.
Publ.—Publication.
P.W.D.—Public Works Department.
R.C.—Royal Commission.
Registrn.—Registration.
Registr.—Registrar.
Regt.—Regiment.
Rest.—Resident.
Retd.—Retired.
Rev.—Revenue.
R.I.E. Coll.—Royal Indian Engineering College, Cooper's Hill.
Sch.—School.
Sec.—Secretary.
Sectt.—Secretariat.
Senr.—Senior.
Serv.—Service.
Settlt.—Settlement.
Subord.—Subordinate.
S.p.t.—Substantive pro tempore.
Supt.—Superintendent.
Suptg.—Superintending.
Surgn.—Surgeon.
T.C.E. Coll.—Thomason Civil Engineering College, Roorkee.
Topogr.—Topographical.
Transfd.—Transferred.
Transld.—Translated.
Transln.—Translation.
Translr.—Translator.
Transpn.—Transportation.
Trigonl.—Trigonometrical.
Uncov.—Uncovenanted.
Univ.—University.
U.S. List—Unemployed Supernumerary List.
Vaccn.—Vaccination.
Vety.—Veterinary.

RECORD OF SERVICES.

A-ABABRELTON, ROBERT RELTON DE RELTON, I.S.O., M.A., F.S.S., late India Office (b. 1st July, 1875).—Educ. at Downing Coll., Camb.; apptd. second div. clerk, War Office, April, 1900; 2nd class clerk, India Office, 15th Sept., 1903; transfed. to central telegraph section, July, 1920; asst. editor of telegraph codes, April, 1921; higher clerical officer, April, 1922; actg. as officer in charge, central telegraph section, May to Oct., 1923; supt., tel. and mails branch, Oct., 1931; I.S.O., June, 1938; retd., July, 1938.

ABBAS ALI KHAN, SAHIB BAHADUR, M.A., LL.B., Barr. (ch. presdy. magte., Madras) (b. 19th April, 1891).—Joined the service as offg. ch. presdy. magte., 26th May, 1928; confd., Nov., 1928.

ABBAS, ASGHAR (see Asghar Abbas).

ABBASI, MUHAMMAD WAHAJUDDIN, Indian C.S. (jt. mag., United Provs.) (b. 4th July, 1905).—Educ. at Lucknow University and Jesus College, Cambridge; joined the service 11th Oct., 1930, arrived in India Nov., 1930, and served in the United Provs. as asst. collr. and mag.

ABBOTT, JOHN, B.A. (Oxon), late Indian C.S. (b. 28th Sept., 1884).—Educ. at Manchester Gr. Sch., and Corpus Christi Coll., Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1908; arrived, 25th Nov., 1909, and served in Bombay as asst. collr. and asst. settlt. offr.; offg. collr. and dist. mag. and pol. agent, Oct., 1918, Feb., 1920, Dec., 1923, and Jan., 1928; confd. Jan., 1928; retd., Dec., 1932.

ABBOTT, STUART EVELYN, B.A., Indian C.S. (asst. commr., Punjab) (b. 21st Aug., 1910).—Educ. at Rugby and Balliol College, Oxford; apptd. after exam. of 1933; arrived in India, Nov., 1934, and served in Punjab as asst. commr.

ABDUL AHAD, SHAIKH, B.A., LL.B., Indian Police (b. 14th Jan., 1899).—Joined the service as asst. supt., Punjab, on 15th July, 1923; offg. supt., Feb., 1932.

ABDUL ALI, ABUL FAIZ MUHAMMAD KHAN BAHADUR, M.A., Bengal Civil Service (keeper of records of Govt. of India) (b. 1st Jan., 1884).—Joined the service, 24th August, 1906, as dep. mag. and dep. collr.; asst. sec., General dept., Bengal, Nov., 1915, to April, 1916; sub-divl. officer, April, 1916, to Sept., 1918; placed on special duty in the pol. dept., Bengal, as special Muhammadan press censor, Sept., 1918, to March, 1919; police mag. Alipore, Sept., 1921, to March, 1922; offg. keeper of the records of the govt. of India, April, 1922; confd. 1st Sept., 1923.

ABDUL ALI, HAMID MOIZUDIN, B.A., late Indian C.S. (b. 5th Jan., 1880).—Educ. at St. Xavier's High Sch., Bombay, Bombay Univ., and Emmanuel Coll., Cambridge; apptd. after exam. of 1903; arrived, 2nd Dec., 1904, and served in Sind as asst. collr.

and mag.; supt. land records and registr., Sind, Dec., 1909; manager, Sind encumbered estates, June, 1913, to May, 1914; offg. collr. and dist. mag., Jan., 1919; ditto, Oct., 1923; confirmed. Feb., 1925; retd., Feb., 1937.

ABDUL ALI KHAN, Indian Forest Service (dep. consvr., Madras) (b. 7th Nov., 1904).—Joined the service, 14th Oct., 1927; dep. consvr., Nov., 1931.

ABDUL AZIZ, KHAN BAHADUR, SHAIKH, C.I.E., O.B.E., late Indian Police (b. 1st June, 1878).—Joined the service, 1st June, 1899; King's police medal, Jan., 1916; supt., May, 1924; O.B.E., June, 1925; C.I.E., June, 1930; retd., June, 1934.

ABDUL AZIZ, KHAN BAHADUR, MIAN, C.B.E., M.A., late Punjab Civil Service (b. 27th Jan., 1881).—Joined the service, 1st Nov., 1902; dep. commr., Sept., 1923; C.B.E., June, 1930; jt. secty., Indian round table conf., London, Nov., 1930-Jan., 1931; offg. commr., Oct., 1933; offg. finl. commr. (rev.) and secty. to govt., Punjab, rev. dept., Oct., 1935; retd., May, 1936.

ABDUL AZIZ KHAN, Khan Bahadur, Indian State Ry's. (transpn. supt., E.I. rly.) (b. 4th Dec., 1891).—Joined the service, 1st Oct., 1913; traffic manager, Mysore Ry's., June, 1922; offg. agent and secty. to Govt. for ry's., Mysore, May, 1923; confd., Apl., 1927.

ABDUL AZIZ, MUHAMMAD, KHAN BAHADUR, C.I.E., B.A., Indian Service of Engineers (suptg. engr., United Provs.) (b. 13th Dec., 1884).—Joined the service, 14th Oct., 1908; asst. engr., Oct., 1909; exec. engr., Oct., 1917; offg. suptg. engr., Feb., 1931; confd., Dec., 1933; offg. ch. engr., and jt. secty., irrign. bch., U.P., Aug., 1936; suptg. engr., Apl., 1937; C.I.E., May, 1937.

ABDUL GHAFOOR KHAN, KHAN BAHADUR, B.A., M.Sc. (Delhi), M.Sc. (Tech.), Manch. M.I.Mech.E., M.I.E.E., M.I.E. (Ind.) (dep. dir. inspection) (b. 2nd Aug., 1893).—Joined service 14th Feb., 1921, as tempy. engr., P.W.D., Delhi; Mar., 1923, elec. inspr. (in addn.), asst. dir. of insp. Indian Stores Dept., June, 1923; contr. of insp., Apl., 1930; dep. dir. of insp., Apl., 1930.

ABDUL GHAFFUR, Indian Service of Engrs. (exec. engr., Punjab) (b. 1st Nov., 1899).—Joined the service as appr. engr., 3rd Nov., 1921; asst. engr. (provl.), Nov., 1922; confd. Jan., 1923; apptd. to the I.S.E. as asst. exec. engr., Jan., 1930; exec. engr., Jan., 1937.

ABDUL HAFEEZ, MUHAMMAD, M.A., Indian Finance Dept. (b. 30th Sept., 1883).—Joined the service, 12th Feb., 1907; asst. acct. genl., Nov., 1907; dep. acct.-genl., April, 1914; dep. auditor-genl., Jan., 1927; comptr., Assam, Mar., 1930; offg. acct. genl., C.P., Feb., 1931; dep. ch. auditor, E.I. Ry., Nov., 1931; offg. acct.-genl., Central Provs., Jan., 1932; acct.-genl., Bihar and Orissa, Nov., 1933; ch. auditor, N.W. Ry., June, 1936.

ABDUL HALIM, MUNSHI, late Judicial dept., United Provs. (b. Mar., 1877).—Joined the service as offg. munsfib., 23rd Jan., 1906; confd., Sept., 1908; sub. judge (s.p.t.), July, 1919; confd., Sept., 1919; offg. dist. and sess. judge, Apl., 1927; confd., Nov., 1930; retd., Mar., 1932.

ABDUL HAMID, KHAN BAHADUR, B.Sc., M.B., B.S.(Allied.), D.P.H.(Eng.), Public Health Dept., United Provs. (asst. dir. of Public Health) (b. Apl., 1893).—Joined the service as health officer, 1 June, 1926.

ABDUL HAMID KHAN, Indian Forest Service (dep. consvr., Madras) (b. 28th Oct., 1906).—Joined the service as asst. consvr., 13th Nov., 1931; dep. consvr., Nov., 1936.

ABDUL HAMID, SHEIKH, M.A., Punjab Civil Service (b. 16th Oct., 1891).—Extra asst. commr., Punjab, Jan., 1916; dep. commr., criminal tribes, insp.-genl., reformatory schools and reclaim. officer, Dec., 1935.

ABDUL KAIYUM KHAN, KHAN, Indian Police (supt., Punjab) (b. 5th June, 1898).—Joined the service as asst. supt. on 12th April, 1920; offg. supt., June, 1925; confd. Nov., 1926.

ABDUL KARIM, MAULAVI, B.Sc., Indian Service of Engrs. (exec. engr., Bihar) (b. 26th Aug., 1893).—Joined the service, 13th Oct., 1917; asst. engr., Oct., 1918; asst. exec. engr., June, 1921; exec. engr., Oct., 1927.

ABDUL MAJID KHAN, B.A., LL.B., Bombay Civil Service, Judl. Branch (asst. judge) (b. 12th Dec., 1884).—Held various judl. appts. from Oct., 1913; sub. judge, 16th April, 1918; offg. asst. judge, June, 1928; confd., Oct., 1931; offg. dist. and sess. judge, June, 1931.

ABDUL MAJID, KHAN BAHADUR, O.B.E., late Indian Police Service (b. 16th June, 1879).—Held non-gazetted appt. from 10th March, 1898, to 8th July, 1910; inspr., July, 1910; dep. supt., Feb., 1912; asst. dir., intell. bureau, home dept., govt. of India (prov.), July, 1918; confd. May, 1922; O.B.E., Jan., 1920; appd. to I.P.S. as supt., Nov., 1925; retd., Dec., 1929.

ABDUL QADIR, SIR, KT., KHAN BAHADUR, Barr. (adviser to the Sec. of State for India) Apptd. addl. judge, high ct., Lahore, 20th Feb., 1930, Feb., 1932; and Feb., 1934; member of Council of India, July, 1934; adviser to the sec. of state for India, Apl., 1937.

ABDUL QAIYUM AHMAD KHAN, Khan, Bahadur, B.A., Indian Police (supt., Punjab) (b. 10th Oct., 1887).—Joined the service, 27th Sept. 1912, as inspr. of police; apptd. to I.P. as supt., Apl., 1935.

ABDUL QAIYUM KHAN, KHAN, BARA KHAN KHEL, Indian Police (supt.) (b. 5th July, 1898).—Apptd. asst. supt., Punjab, on probn., Apl., 1920; Confd., Feb., 1922; supt., Nov., 1926.

ABDUL RAOOF, SIR MUHAMMAD, Kt., Khan Bahadur, Barr.; apptd. offg. temp. addl. judge, Punjab chief ct., Dec., 1918; judge, Punjab high ct., June, 1920 to Oct., 1925; Kt., Jan., 1926.

ABDUL RASHID, MIAN, M.A., Barr. Judl. Dept., Punjab (judge, high ct., Lahore).—Apptd. asst. legal remembr., 5th Mar., 1925; offg. judge, high ct., Lahore, May, 1933; addl. ditto, Oct., 1933; judge, ditto, Mar., 1937.

ABDUL WAHID, B.Sc., Indian Service of Engrs. (exec. engr., Central Provs.) (b. Apl., 1894).—Joined the service, 15th Oct., 1917; asst. engr., Oct., 1928; asst. exec. engr., Nov., 1918; offg. exec. engr., Mar., 1926; confd., Oct., 1927; on service in Bhopal state, Aug., 1933.

ABDULLAH ABU SAIVID, KHAN BAHADUR, M.A., Indian Educl. Service (b. June, 1885).—Apptd. princpl., govt. Coll., Madras, Sylhet and prof'r. Murarichand coll., Sylhet, 5th Feb., 1921; princpl., Murarichand coll., July, 1933.

ABDULLAH MUHAMMAD (see Muhammad A.)

ABDULLAH, MUHAMMAD ABOO (see Muhammad Aboo Abdullah.)

ABDUR RAHIM, Sir, K.C.S.I., M.A., Barr.—Apptd. judge, Madras High Court, 24th July, 1908; member, Royal commn. on public services in India, 1912; Kt., June, 1919; member of govrs. exec. council, Bengal, Jan., 1921, to Dec., 1925; K.C.S.I., June, 1925; presd., Indian legis. assembly, 1935; author of 'Principles of Muhammadan Jurisprudence.'

ABDUR RAHIM, B.A., Indian C.S. (asst. commr., Punjab) (b. 14th Sept., 1908).—Educ. at Punjab University and Peterhouse, Cambridge; joined the service, 12th Oct., 1932; arrived in India, Dec., 1932, and served in the Punjab as asst. commr.

ABDUR RAHIM KHAN, Capt. I.A. (Indian Political Service (b. 9th Nov., 1898).—First commn. dated 17th July, 1920; in milty. employ prior to 31st May, 1925; asst. collr., Jhansi, June, 1925; asst. mag., Moradabad, Nov., 1925; asst. commr., Bannu, Apl., 1926; ditto, Thal, Nov., 1927; ditto, Kohat, Nov., 1928; asst. pol. agent, N. Waziristan, Feb., 1929; 1st asst. to resdt., Baroda, May, 1929; asst. pol. agent, S. Waziristan, Apl., 1930; city mag., Peshawar, Apl., 1931; addl. dist. and sess. judge, Peshawar and Hazara, Nov., 1933; dist. and sess. judge, Dera Ismail Khan, Mar., 1934; ditto, Peshawar, Oct., 1935; pol. agent, S. Waziristan, Nov., 1937; dep. commr., Dera Ismail Khan, Feb., 1938.

ABDUR RAHIM SAHIB, KHAN BAHADUR, B.A., late Indian Educl. Service (b. 16th July, 1883).—Apptd. to the I.E.S. as inspector of schools, Madras, 23rd Feb., 1921; addl. dep. dir., public instrn., July, 1921; offg. dist. educt. offr., Mar., 1926; confd., Apl., 1929; dep. dir. pub. instrn., Madras, Dec., 1935; retd., July, 1938.

ABDURRAHMAN, KHAN BAHADUR KHWAJA, O.B.E., M.B., Ch.B., D.P.H., late Dept. of Public Health, Punjab (b. 21st Aug., 1881).—Joined the service, 2nd March, 1910; dep. sany. commr., Nov., 1915; asst. dir., Jan., 1923; offg. dir., May, 1930; and again, July, 1932; confd., Dec., 1933; O.B.E., June, 1934; retd., Aug., 1936.

ABDUR RAHMAN, KHAN BAHADUR, SHEIKH, Indian Service of Engrs. (exec. engr., Punjab) (b. 13th Feb., 1884).—Joined the service, 20th Sept., 1905; asst. engr., Oct., 1906; exec. engr., Oct., 1914; offg. suptg.-engr. and secty., rural sanitary board, Punjab, Apl., 1927.

ABDUR RAHMAN, SIR MUHAMMED, KHAN BAHADUR (b. 5th Oct., 1888).—(Judge high ct., Madras) Dean of faculty of law, Delhi Univ., 1927-34; vice-Chancellor, Delhi Univ., 1930-34; apptd. judge high ct., Madras, Aug., 1937.

ABDUS SALAM SAIYID, Income-tax Dept., Madras (asst. commr. of income-tax) (b. 15th Dec., 1890).—Joined the service, May, 1914; held non-gaz. appts.; inspector, 4th grade (Salt, etc., Dept.), Apl., 1920; income-tax offr., Apl., 1924; confd. as asst. commr. of income-tax, Dec., 1935.

ABDUS SAMAD KHAN, M.Sc., Indian Educl. Service (dep. dir. of public instn., Bihar) (b. 1st Apl., 1891).—Joined the service, 4th Dec., 1919; inspr. of schools, May, 1922; profr. of chemistry, science coll., Patna, Jan., 1928; offg. princpl., Greer Bhumihar Coll., Muzaffarpur, Jan., 1936. dep. dir. of public instn., Aug., 1936.

ABELL, GEORGE EDMOND BRACKENBURY, Indian Civil Service (asst. commr. Punjab) (b. 22nd June, 1904).—Educated at Marlborough and Corpus Christi College, Oxford; joined the service, 10th Oct., 1928, arrived, Nov., 1928, and served in the Punjab as asst. commr.; offg. dep. regr., co-op. soc., Mar., 1932; confd., July, 1932; settlement offr., Oct., 1935.

ABERNETHY, JOHN, Indian Police (asst. supt.) (b. 12th June, 1910).—Joined the service as proby. asst. supt., Bombay, 25th Nov., 1930; confd., Sept., 1932; offg. dist. supt., June, 1933.

ABIGAIL, REGINALD PHILIP, Indian C.S. (dep. commr., Burma) (b. 11th Aug., 1892).—Educ. at Trent Coll., Derbyshire and Bedford Modern Sch.; on mil. service from May, 1918, to Mar., 1923; joined the service 29th Oct., 1923, and served in Burma as asst. commr.; offg. under secy. to govt., April, 1925; dep. commr., Jan., 1931.

ABRAHAM, EDGAR GARTON FURTADO, C.B., B.A., late Indian C.S.
